text: Heaps Chapter 17 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved The ADT Heap • A heap is a complete binary tree that either is – Empty or … – Whose root contains a value ≥ each of its children and has heaps as its subtrees • It is a special binary tree … different in that – It is ordered in a weaker sense – it will always be a complete binary tree © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved The ADT Heap FIGURE 17-1 (a) A maxheap and (b) a minheap © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved The ADT Heap FIGURE 17-2 UML diagram for the class Heap © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Array-Based Implementation of a Heap Figure 17-3 A complete binary tree and its array-based implementation © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Given any node items[i], you can easily locate both of its children and its parent: • Its left child, if it exists, is items[2 * i + 1] • Its right child, if it exists, is items[2 * i + 2] • Its parent, if it exists, is items[(i - 1) / 2] Algorithms for Array-Based Heap Operations • Assume following private data members – items: an array of heap items – itemCount: an integer equal to the number of items in the heap – maxItems: an integer equal to the maximum capacity of the heap © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Algorithms for Array-Based Heap Operations FIGURE 17-4 Disjoint heaps after removing the heap’s root © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Algorithms for Array-Based Heap Operations © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Remove root item (10) from a heap. 1. remove the last node of the tree and place its item in the root 2. If it is not a heap, then it is a semiheap. Recursively, trickle down the item in the root until it reaches a node in which it will not be out of place (heapify) Removal efficiency. • This item travels down a single path from the root to, at worst, a leaf. • The number of array items it must swap is no greater than the height of the tree. • Thus, remove is O(log n), which is in fact quite efficient. Algorithms for Array-Based Heap Operations Remove an arbitrary item (9) from a heap (unknown index). 1. remove the last node of the tree and place its item with the item that is to be removed 2. If it is not a heap, then it is a semiheap. Recursively, trickle down the item in the root until it reaches a node in which it will not be out of place (heapify) Removal efficiency. • Find the arbitrary node which is O(n) • Swap with the last node which is O(1) • Remove the last node which is O(1) • Heapify (up or down) which is O(log n) • Thus, to remove an arbitrary node is O(n + log n), which is O(n). 9 6 3 2 5 10 5 6 3 2 10 Algorithms for Array-Based Heap Operations FIGURE 17-6 Recursive calls to heapRebuild © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Algorithms for Array-Based Heap Operations FIGURE 17-5 Adding 15 to a heap © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Add an item (15) to a heap (opposite of remove). 1. Insert new item at the bottom of the tree 2. Recursively trickle up to its proper place (the parent of the node in items[i] is always stored in items[(i - 1) / 2] —unless, of course, the node is the root). Add efficiency. • This item travels down a single path from a leaf to the root, at worst. • The number of array items it must swap is no greater than the height of the tree. • Thus, add is O(log n), which is in fact quite efficient. Transforming Binary Tree to Heap FIGURE 17-8 Array and its corresponding complete binary tree © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Transforming Binary Tree to Heap FIGURE 17-9 Transforming an array into a heap © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved function buildMaxHeap(array): n = length of array # Step 1: Start from the last non-leaf node and move upwards for i from n/2 - 1 down to 0: # from index 2, 1, 0 heapRebuild(array, n, i) function heapRebuild(array, n, i): largest = i # initialize largest to root leftChild = 2 * i + 1 # index 5, array[leftChild] = 10 rightChild = 2 * i + 2 # none # Step 2: Check if the left child exists and is greater than the current largest if leftChild < n and array[leftChild] > array[largest]: largest = leftChild # Step 3: Check if the right child exists and is greater than the current largest if rightChild < n and array[rightChild] > array[largest]: largest = rightChild # Step 4: If largest is not the current node, swap and heapRebuild the affected subtree if largest != i: swap(array[i], array[largest]) heapRebuild(array, n, largest) Heap Sort FIGURE 17-10 Heap sort partitions an array into two regions © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved heap sort algorithm 1. Transform array into heap 2. For each item from heap a. Swap the root (largest item) with last item in the heap Swap(myArray[0], myArray[last]) b. Transform heap region to heap c. Decrement last /* reduce heap region/increase sorted region */ Heap Sort FIGURE 17-11 A trace of heap sort © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved heap sort algorithm 1. Transform array into heap 2. For each item from heap a. Swap the root (largest item) with last item in the heap Swap(myArray[0], myArray[last]) b. Transform heap region to heap c. Decrement last /* reduce heap region/increase sorted region */ Heap Sort FIGURE 17-11 A trace of heap sort © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved heap sort algorithm 1. Transform array into heap 2. For each item from heap a. Swap the root (largest item) with last item in the heap Swap(myArray[0], myArray[last]) b. Transform heap region to heap c. Decrement last /* reduce heap region/increase sorted region */ Heap Sort • Heap sort converts an array into a heap to locate the array’s largest item. This step enables the heap sort to sort an array in an efficient manner. • Heap sort, like merge sort, has good worst-case and average- case behaviors – O(n * log n) // applies to the worst and average cases • Heap sort has an advantage over merge sort in that it does not require a second array. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved End Chapter 17 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Appendix Chapter 17 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Heap Implementation of the ADT Priority Queue • Heap versus a binary search tree – If you know maximum number of items in the priority queue, heap is the better implementation. • Finite, distinct priority values – Many items likely have same priority value – Place in same order as encountered © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. Array-Based Implementation of a Heap Figure 17-3 A complete binary tree and its array-based implementation © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Given any node items[i], you can easily locate both of its children and its parent: • Its left child, if it exists, is items[2 * i + 1] • Its right child, if it exists, is items[2 * i + 2] • Its parent, if it exists, is items[(i - 1) / 2]

Algorithms for Array-Based Heap Operations • Assume following private data members – items: an array of heap items – itemCount: an integer equal to the number of items in the heap – maxItems: an integer equal to the maximum capacity of the heap © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. Algorithms for Array-Based Heap Operations FIGURE 17-4 Disjoint heaps after removing the heap’s root © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Algorithms for Array-Based Heap Operations © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Remove root item (10) from a heap. 1. remove the last node of the tree and place its item in the root 2. If it is not a heap, then it is a semiheap. Recursively, trickle down the item in the root until it reaches a node in which it will not be out of place (heapify) Removal efficiency. • This item travels down a single path from the root to, at worst, a leaf. • The number of array items it must swap is no greater than the height of the tree. • Thus, remove is O(log n), which is in fact quite efficient.

Algorithms for Array-Based Heap Operations Remove an arbitrary item (9) from a heap (unknown index). 1. remove the last node of the tree and place its item with the item that is to be removed 2. If it is not a heap, then it is a semiheap. Recursively, trickle down the item in the root until it reaches a node in which it will not be out of place (heapify) Removal efficiency. • Find the arbitrary node which is O(n) • Swap with the last node which is O(1) • Remove the last node which is O(1) • Heapify (up or down) which is O(log n) • Thus, to remove an arbitrary node is O(n + log n), which is O(n). 9 6 3 2 5 10 5 6 3 2 10

Algorithms for Array-Based Heap Operations FIGURE 17-5 Adding 15 to a heap © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Add an item (15) to a heap (opposite of remove). 1. Insert new item at the bottom of the tree 2. Recursively trickle up to its proper place (the parent of the node in items[i] is always stored in items[(i - 1) / 2] —unless, of course, the node is the root). Add efficiency. • This item travels down a single path from a leaf to the root, at worst. • The number of array items it must swap is no greater than the height of the tree. • Thus, add is O(log n), which is in fact quite efficient.

Transforming Binary Tree to Heap FIGURE 17-9 Transforming an array into a heap © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved function buildMaxHeap(array): n = length of array # Step 1: Start from the last non-leaf node and move upwards for i from n/2 - 1 down to 0: # from index 2, 1, 0 heapRebuild(array, n, i) function heapRebuild(array, n, i): largest = i # initialize largest to root leftChild = 2 * i + 1 # index 5, array[leftChild] = 10 rightChild = 2 * i + 2 # none # Step 2: Check if the left child exists and is greater than the current largest if leftChild < n and array[leftChild] > array[largest]: largest = leftChild # Step 3: Check if the right child exists and is greater than the current largest if rightChild < n and array[rightChild] > array[largest]: largest = rightChild # Step 4: If largest is not the current node, swap and heapRebuild the affected subtree if largest != i: swap(array[i], array[largest]) heapRebuild(array, n, largest)

Heap Sort FIGURE 17-10 Heap sort partitions an array into two regions © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved heap sort algorithm 1. Transform array into heap 2. For each item from heap a. Swap the root (largest item) with last item in the heap Swap(myArray[0], myArray[last]) b. Transform heap region to heap c. Decrement last /* reduce heap region/increase sorted region */

Heap Sort FIGURE 17-11 A trace of heap sort © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved heap sort algorithm 1. Transform array into heap 2. For each item from heap a. Swap the root (largest item) with last item in the heap Swap(myArray[0], myArray[last]) b. Transform heap region to heap c. Decrement last /* reduce heap region/increase sorted region */

Heap Sort FIGURE 17-11 A trace of heap sort © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved heap sort algorithm 1. Transform array into heap 2. For each item from heap a. Swap the root (largest item) with last item in the heap Swap(myArray[0], myArray[last]) b. Transform heap region to heap c. Decrement last /* reduce heap region/increase sorted region */

Heap Sort • Heap sort converts an array into a heap to locate the array’s largest item. This step enables the heap sort to sort an array in an efficient manner. • Heap sort, like merge sort, has good worst-case and average- case behaviors – O(n * log n) // applies to the worst and average cases • Heap sort has an advantage over merge sort in that it does not require a second array. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
